usecase.name: Tampering
usecase.desc: Tampering refers to unauthorized modification of data or systems. Attackers may alter data in transit or modify software components to introduce vulnerabilities or compromise the integrity of the system.

threat.name: Attackers manipulate the server in unintended ways, e.g., injection attacks
threat.desc:
General Threat Description

Injection attacks allow attackers to manipulate a server by executing unauthorized commands or accessing data through the server's interfaces. These attacks exploit vulnerabilities in the server's input validation mechanisms to inject malicious code or commands, leading to a wide range of malicious activities. The most common forms include SQL injection, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Command Injection, and Remote File Inclusion (RFI).

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors

Threat agents include cybercriminals, hacking groups, and sometimes malicious insiders with varying levels of technical expertise. The primary attack vectors involve submitting crafted input into web forms, query parameters, or any input fields that are improperly sanitized by the server. This can be achieved via web applications, API endpoints, or any interface that processes user-supplied data.

Impacts

The impacts of injection attacks are severe and varied, ranging from unauthorized access to sensitive data, complete system compromise, denial of service, to the exposure of confidential information. These attacks can lead to significant financial losses, damage to reputation, and legal consequences, especially if they result in the breach of data protection regulations.

Example Attack Scenarios
  • An attacker uses an RFI vulnerability to include a malicious file from a remote server, executing a script that gives them unauthorized access to the web server's file system.
  • Through a SQL injection attack, a cybercriminal manipulates a query form on a website to gain access to the database, extracting sensitive user information, including passwords and personal data.
  • Exploiting a path traversal flaw, an attacker manipulates the web application to access files and directories stored outside the web root folder, leading to the exposure of sensitive configuration files.
  • An attacker sends crafted inputs that cause the application to generate an error message, revealing details about the underlying database structure, which they then exploit to perform more targeted attacks.

reference.name: CWE-706: Use of Incorrectly-Resolved Name or Reference
Injection Attacks

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - T1059 - Command and Scripting Interpreter||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1190 - Exploit Public-Facing Application||ATT&CK Enterprise - TA0001 - Initial Access||ATT&CK Enterprise - TA0005 - Defense Evasion||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1659 - Content Injection

threat.name: Attackers manipulate SSL/TLS weaknesses, e.g., Heartbleed
threat.desc:
General Threat Description

SSL/TLS weaknesses exploit vulnerabilities in the implementation or configuration of the encryption protocols used to secure internet communications. These weaknesses can lead to various security issues, such as the decryption, modification, and interception of supposed-to-be secure data. Notable vulnerabilities like Heartbleed have highlighted the risks associated with outdated or improperly configured encryption protocols and underscored the importance of using up-to-date and strong ciphers, libraries, and configurations.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors

Threat agents include nation-states, cybercriminal organizations, and individual hackers interested in intercepting or tampering with encrypted data. The attack vectors vary but often involve exploiting known vulnerabilities in specific versions of SSL/TLS protocols or their implementations, such as OpenSSL. Attackers may also leverage weaknesses in cryptographic practices, such as the use of weak or deprecated encryption ciphers.

Impacts

The impacts of exploiting SSL/TLS weaknesses can be profound, affecting the confidentiality and integrity of communications. This may lead to sensitive information being exposed, such as login credentials, personal data, or financial information. It can also undermine the trust users place in the security of online transactions and communications, potentially leading to financial loss and reputational damage for the entities involved.

Example Attack Scenarios
  • An attacker exploits the Heartbleed vulnerability in an outdated version of OpenSSL to extract data from the memory of a web server, including private keys, user passwords, and personal information.
  • Through the use of a weak cipher, an attacker performs a man-in-the-middle attack, decrypting and altering data being exchanged between a client and a server without detection.
  • An attacker exploits vulnerabilities in the SSLv3 protocol, such as POODLE, to downgrade the encryption security and intercept the data being transferred over what is assumed to be a secure connection.

reference.name: What are TLS/SSL Cipher Suites and how to order them

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - TA0005 - Defense Evasion||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1600 - Weaken Encryption||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1588 - Obtain Capabilities

usecase.name: Spoofing
usecase.desc: Spoofing involves an attacker pretending to be someone else by falsifying data or identity. This can include spoofing email addresses, IP addresses, or user identities to gain unauthorized access to systems or information.

threat.name: Attackers intercept and tamper with data
threat.desc:
General Threat Description

Attackers intercepting and tampering with data involve unauthorized access to and alteration of the communication between a client and a web server. This threat exploits vulnerabilities in the web server, its configuration, or the network, allowing attackers to read, modify, or redirect data. The lack of encryption or improper encryption practices often facilitates these attacks, significantly undermining the confidentiality and integrity of the data exchanged.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors

The primary threat agents include cybercriminals, espionage groups, and malicious insiders, utilizing techniques such as Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks, packet sniffing, and exploiting server vulnerabilities. Attack vectors can range from compromised network equipment, such as routers, to software vulnerabilities in the web server itself or the protocols it uses. Attackers may also leverage unsecured or poorly secured Wi-Fi networks to intercept communications.

Impacts

The impacts of data interception and tampering can be severe, leading to the compromise of sensitive information, such as user credentials, financial data, and personal information. It can also result in the dissemination of malware, the alteration of transaction data, and loss of trust among users and clients. Furthermore, such attacks could have legal and regulatory implications for the affected organization, especially if it leads to a breach of data protection laws.

Example Attack Scenarios
  • An attacker performing a MitM attack intercepts and alters the communication between a user's browser and an online banking website, redirecting a transaction to a different account.
  • Through packet sniffing on an unencrypted Wi-Fi network, a cybercriminal captures login credentials that a user submits to a web application, leading to unauthorized access to the user’s account.
  • Exploiting vulnerabilities in the web server’s configuration, an attacker modifies the contents of a message being sent from the client to the server, injecting malicious code that compromises the server.
  • An attacker intercepts data being transmitted over an insecure connection and inserts a malicious redirect into the session, leading users to a phishing site designed to capture their credentials.

reference.name: OWASP Top 10:2021 - Cryptographic Failures

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - T1036 - Masquerading||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1001 - Data Obfuscation||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1056 - Input Capture||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1557 - Adversary-in-the-Middle

usecase.name: Information Disclosure
usecase.desc: Information disclosure occurs when sensitive data is exposed to unauthorized parties. This can include unauthorized access to confidential information, such as personal data, financial records, or intellectual property.

threat.name: Attackers gain access to information about the server, e.g., verbose errors or misconfiguration
threat.desc:
General Threat Description

This threat involves attackers gaining unauthorized access to sensitive information about the server, such as software versions, configuration details, and even secrets like passwords or encryption keys. This can occur through various means, including verbose error messages, misconfigurations, unsecured network communications, and vulnerable server functions. The exposure of such information can significantly weaken the security posture of the server, making it more susceptible to targeted attacks.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors

Threat agents can include external attackers, malicious insiders, or automated bots scanning for vulnerabilities. Attack vectors involve exploiting misconfigured server settings that expose too much information, inducing errors that reveal sensitive information, intercepting unencrypted traffic to read data transmitted between the server and its clients, and exploiting vulnerabilities in the server's functions that may leak information about its internal state or operations.

Impacts

The impacts of information leakage are multi-faceted. They can lead to a loss of confidentiality, enabling attackers to conduct more targeted and effective attacks. For instance, knowledge about specific software versions can allow attackers to exploit known vulnerabilities. Additionally, the exposure of sensitive information can lead to compliance issues, legal consequences, and a loss of trust from users and clients.

Example Attack Scenarios
  • An attacker sends unexpected input to the server, triggering a verbose error message that includes database connection details or file paths on the server.
  • Through misconfigured HTTP headers, an attacker discovers the exact version of the web server software being used, enabling them to exploit known vulnerabilities specific to that version.
  • An attacker captures unencrypted traffic between the server and a client, gaining access to sensitive information transmitted in plaintext, such as user credentials or personal data.
  • Exploiting a memory vulnerability in the server application, an attacker is able to read parts of the server memory that contain sensitive information, such as user session tokens or secret keys.

reference.name: Information disclosure vulnerabilities

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - T1087 - Account Discovery||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1190 - Exploit Public-Facing Application||ATT&CK Enterprise - TA0001 - Initial Access||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1082 - System Information Discovery||ATT&CK Enterprise - TA0007 - Discovery

threat.name: Insufficient logging
threat.desc:
General Threat Description

Insufficient logging is a critical security oversight where an environment fails to adequately record events, or the logs lack the necessary detail to identify and understand security incidents. This deficiency hampers the detection of malicious activities, compromises the identification of attack patterns, and undermines forensic analysis capabilities. It results in prolonged detection and response times to security incidents, thereby increasing the risk and potential impact of breaches.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors

Threat agents exploiting insufficient logging range from external hackers to malicious insiders. Without comprehensive logging, these actors can operate undetected within an environment, exploiting vulnerabilities and extracting sensitive information. The lack of detailed logs also means that even after detection, tracing the attackers' steps and understanding the full scope of the compromise is challenging, if not impossible.

Impacts

The primary impact of insufficient logging is the extended dwell time of attackers within a network, increasing the potential for damage and data loss. It also significantly hinders incident response and forensic investigations, leading to delayed responses and potentially incorrect assessments of incidents. In the long term, insufficient logging can lead to regulatory and compliance issues, reputational damage, and financial losses due to the inability to defend against or quickly respond to attacks.

Example Attack Scenarios
  • An attacker exploits a vulnerability in a web application to gain unauthorized access to the system. Due to insufficient logging, the intrusion goes unnoticed for an extended period, allowing the attacker to extract sensitive data undetected.
  • A malicious insider abuses their access rights to exfiltrate company data. The lack of detailed access and activity logs prevents the identification of the insider as the source of the breach.
  • Due to insufficient logging, a company fails to notice the indicators of a ransomware attack in its early stages. This oversight allows the ransomware to encrypt critical data and spread across the network, leading to significant operational disruption.
  • In a cloud environment, the default logging settings are not adjusted to capture detailed event logs. As a result, when an attacker compromises cloud-based resources, the attack goes undetected for weeks, causing extensive data loss and system compromise.

reference.name: Comprehensive guide to Insufficient Logging & Monitoring

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - TA0005 - Defense Evasion||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1562 - Impair Defenses

usecase.name: Elevation of Privilege
usecase.desc: Elevation of privilege involves an attacker gaining higher levels of access or permissions than intended. By exploiting vulnerabilities, attackers can escalate their privileges within a system to perform unauthorized actions or access sensitive data.

threat.name: Attackers carry out cross-service attacks
threat.desc:
General Threat Description

Cross-service attacks exploit vulnerabilities or misconfigurations in one service to compromise or affect another within the same network or environment. These attacks leverage the interconnectedness and interdependencies of services in a modern computing environment, targeting the lack of proper segmentation, insecure service integrations, or misconfigurations. The ultimate goal of such attacks is often to 'pivot' within the environment, escalating access from less sensitive to more sensitive areas.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors

Threat agents can range from external attackers to malicious insiders. They use a variety of attack vectors, including exploiting known vulnerabilities in exposed services, taking advantage of insufficient network segmentation, or leveraging stolen credentials to move laterally across services. Other common vectors include the use of phishing attacks to gain initial access or the exploitation of insecure APIs and service interfaces for lateral movement.

Impacts

The impacts of cross-service attacks can be widespread, affecting multiple services and components within the same environment. These can include data breaches, loss of data integrity, service disruption, and unauthorized access to sensitive information. The ability for attackers to pivot across services also increases the potential for more significant damage, as they can leverage the access gained from one compromised service to attack more secure or sensitive areas within the network.

Example Attack Scenarios
  • An attacker exploits a vulnerability in a web server to gain initial access to the network, then uses this foothold to exploit insecure API calls between services to access a database containing sensitive information.
  • Through a misconfigured network, an attacker moves laterally from a compromised external-facing service to an internal service that is not exposed to the internet, leading to unauthorized access to internal-only data and operations.
  • A malicious insider uses their legitimate access to one service to exploit a known vulnerability in another service within the same environment, bypassing security controls and gaining unauthorized access to sensitive information.
  • An attacker exploits a vulnerability in an authentication service to pivot to a payment processing service within the same network, enabling financial fraud or data theft.

reference.name: Cross-Cloud Provider Attacks
Understanding Privilege Escalation and 5 Common Attack Techniques

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - T1189 - Drive-by Compromise||ATT&CK Enterprise - TA0008 - Lateral Movement||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1190 - Exploit Public-Facing Application||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1102 - Web Service||ATT&CK Enterprise - TA0011 - Command and Control||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1505 - Server Software Component

usecase.name: Denial of Service
usecase.desc: Denial of Service attacks aim to disrupt the availability of services or resources to legitimate users. Attackers overwhelm systems with excessive traffic or requests, causing them to become unresponsive or unavailable to users.

threat.name: Attackers cause a service disruption, e.g., DoS
threat.desc:
General Threat Description

Service disruption attacks, including Denial of Service (DoS), aim to make a web server unavailable to its intended users by overwhelming it with a flood of requests or by exploiting its resource management weaknesses. This type of attack targets the availability aspect of security, one of the core components of the CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors

Threat agents include individual hackers, activist groups, or state-sponsored entities using various attack vectors such as botnets to launch distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, or exploiting software vulnerabilities to exhaust server resources. These agents can also perform more sophisticated attacks by exploiting specific vulnerabilities in the server's operating system or applications to cause a denial of service.

Impacts

The impacts of service disruption attacks can be significant, ranging from temporary unavailability of the service, which can lead to loss of revenue and trust among users, to severe resource exhaustion, causing a complete shutdown of the server. In some cases, these attacks can also serve as a smokescreen for more malicious activities, such as data breaches.

Example Attack Scenarios
  • An attacker employs a botnet to send an overwhelming number of requests to a web server, causing a denial of service for legitimate users.
  • Exploiting a vulnerability in the server’s application, an attacker causes the server to allocate excessive memory for certain requests, eventually leading to system crash due to memory exhaustion.
  • An attacker discovers an application flaw that allows them to create an infinite loop, consuming the server's CPU resources and rendering the service unavailable.
  • Through a flaw in the server’s operating system, an attacker repeatedly opens files without closing them, eventually depleting the available file descriptors and preventing the server from serving legitimate requests.

reference.name: CWE-664: Improper Control of a Resource Through its Lifetime
Denial of Service Cheat Sheet
Monitoring Apache web server performance
Understanding Denial-of-Service Attacks

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - TA0040 - Impact||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1110 - Brute Force||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1498 - Network Denial of Service||ATT&CK Enterprise - T1499 - Endpoint Denial of Service

weakness.name: CWE-287
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-287: Improper Authentication When an actor claims to have a given identity, the product does not prove or insufficiently proves that the claim is correct.

weakness.name: CWE-326
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength The product stores or transmits sensitive data using an encryption scheme that is theoretically sound, but is not strong enough for the level of protection required. A weak encryption scheme can be subjected to brute force attacks that have a reasonable chance of succeeding using current attack methods and resources.

weakness.name: CWE-434
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-434: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type The product allows the attacker to upload or transfer files of dangerous types that can be automatically processed within the product's environment.

weakness.name: CWE-494
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-494: Download of Code Without Integrity Check The product downloads source code or an executable from a remote location and executes the code without sufficiently verifying the origin and integrity of the code. An attacker can execute malicious code by compromising the host server, performing DNS spoofing, or modifying the code in transit.

weakness.name: CWE-732
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-732: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource The product specifies permissions for a security-critical resource in a way that allows that resource to be read or modified by unintended actors. When a resource is given a permission setting that provides access to a wider range of actors than required, it could lead to the exposure of sensitive information, or the modification of that resource by unintended parties. This is especially dangerous when the resource is related to program configuration, execution, or sensitive user data. For example, consider a misconfigured storage account for the cloud that can be read or written by a public or anonymous user.

weakness.name: CWE-770
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling The product allocates a reusable resource or group of resources on behalf of an actor without imposing any restrictions on the size or number of resources that can be allocated, in violation of the intended security policy for that actor.

weakness.name: CWE-89
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection') The product constructs all or part of an SQL command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended SQL command when it is sent to a downstream component.

countermeasure.name: Enforce secure file permissions on database files
countermeasure.desc:
Implement and regularly update strict file system permissions on Other SQL Database files to ensure that only authorized users and processes can access or modify them. This control minimizes the risk of unauthorized data exposure or tampering by configuring OS-level permission settings (using tools like chmod and chown on Unix/Linux systems) or by employing centralized configuration management solutions. Developers and DevOps engineers should integrate these practices into deployment and maintenance processes to enforce and audit secure file permissions across the infrastructure.

References:

  • OWASP Secure Coding Practices - Access Control

standard.ref: PR.AC-4: Access permissions and authorizations are managed, incorporating the principles of least privilege and separation of duties
PR.DS-5: Protections against data leaks are implemented

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - M1043 - Credential Access Protection

countermeasure.name: Enforce strong authentication and role-based access control
countermeasure.desc:
Implement and regularly update robust authentication mechanisms and role-based access control for the Other SQL Database to ensure that only verified users with minimal necessary privileges can access and manage the system. This control requires enforcing strong password policies, multi-factor authentication, and defining granular roles based on the principle of least privilege. Developers and DevOps engineers should integrate these measures into the deployment and management processes using centralized identity and access management tools to minimize unauthorized access and reduce the risk of data breaches.

Implementation Steps:

Enforce Strong Authentication:
Configure the database to require complex passwords and multi-factor authentication for all user accounts, leveraging centralized identity providers where possible.

Implement Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):
Define and assign roles with precise privileges that align with user responsibilities, ensuring that each account only has the permissions necessary to perform its tasks.

Centralize Identity Management:
Integrate with an enterprise identity management system (e.g., LDAP, Active Directory) to standardize user provisioning and enforce consistent authentication policies.

Monitor and Audit Access:
Enable comprehensive logging of authentication attempts and access events, and perform regular audits to verify that access controls are properly maintained and adjusted as needed.

References:

  • OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet

standard.ref: Consumers Less Than Under 13 Years of Age (c)
PR.AC-1: Identities and credentials are issued, managed, verified, revoked, and audited for authorized devices, users and processes
PR.AC-6: Identities are proofed and bound to credentials and asserted in interactions

PR.AC-7: Users, devices, and other assets are authenticated (e.g., single-factor, multi-factor) commensurate with the risk of the transaction (e.g., individuals' security and privacy risks and other organizational risks)

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - M1026 - Privileged Account Management

countermeasure.name: Enforce TLS encryption for all connections
countermeasure.desc:
Implement and regularly update TLS encryption for all connections to the Other SQL Database to secure data in transit. This control ensures that every client-to-database and inter-node communication is encrypted using robust TLS protocols (e.g., TLS 1.2/1.3), protecting sensitive data from eavesdropping, interception, and tampering. Developers and DevOps engineers should integrate TLS configuration into the database deployment process, enforce secure cipher suites, and manage certificates via centralized tools to maintain a secure communication environment.

Implementation Steps:

Enable TLS Encryption:
Configure the database server to require TLS for all incoming connections by adjusting the relevant settings in the configuration file.

Configure Secure Cipher Suites:
Enforce the use of modern TLS versions (TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3) and disable deprecated protocols and weak cipher suites to ensure strong encryption.

Manage Certificates:
Use a centralized certificate management system to issue, renew, and revoke TLS certificates, ensuring that all certificates remain current and trusted.

Monitor and Audit:
Regularly review connection logs and perform security audits to verify that TLS is properly implemented and that all communications are encrypted.

References:

  • OWASP Transport Layer Protection Cheat Sheet

standard.ref: DE.AE-1: A baseline of network operations and expected data flows for users and systems is established and managed
ID.AM-3: Organizational communication and data flows are mapped
PR.PT-4: Communications and control networks are protected
SC-16 Transmission of Security and Privacy Attributes

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - M1041 - Encrypt Sensitive Information

countermeasure.name: Harden database configuration and restrict network access
countermeasure.desc:
Implement and regularly update a secure configuration for the Other SQL Database while restricting network access to trusted sources only. This control ensures that the database is configured with secure settings—disabling unnecessary features and services—and is deployed within segmented network environments using firewalls or security groups. Developers and DevOps engineers should integrate these practices into the deployment and maintenance processes to minimize exposure to unauthorized access and reduce potential attack surfaces.

Implementation Steps:

Secure Database Configuration:
Harden the database by disabling default or unnecessary features, enforcing strong authentication, and applying recommended security settings through configuration files or management tools.

Restrict Network Access:
Deploy the database within private network segments or VLANs and configure firewall rules or security groups to allow access only from authorized IP addresses or subnets.

Regular Audits and Updates:
Periodically review the configuration settings and network access policies to ensure they remain effective against emerging threats, and update them as needed.

References:

  • OWASP Secure Architecture - Network Segmentation

standard.ref: DE.CM-1: The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
PR.AC-5: Network integrity is protected (e.g., network segregation, network segmentation)
PR.DS-5: Protections against data leaks are implemented
PR.PT-4: Communications and control networks are protected
SC-22 Architecture and Provisioning for Name/address Resolution Service

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - M1031 - Network Intrusion Prevention

countermeasure.name: Implement rate limiting and resource throttling
countermeasure.desc:
Implement and regularly update rate limiting and resource throttling for the Other SQL Database to mitigate denial-of-service risks and prevent resource exhaustion. This control ensures that the database only processes a defined number of requests per time unit and restricts the consumption of critical resources (CPU, memory, disk I/O) by any single client or operation. Developers and DevOps engineers should integrate these mechanisms into the database deployment using built-in configurations or external middleware, and continuously monitor system performance to adjust thresholds as needed.

Implementation Steps:

Define Rate Limiting Policies:
Establish acceptable thresholds for incoming requests based on typical workload and system capacity.

Implement Rate Limiting Mechanisms:
Utilize built-in database settings, proxy solutions, or middleware to enforce query and connection limits on a per-client basis.

Apply Resource Throttling Controls:
Configure operating system-level resource controls (e.g., cgroups on Linux) or similar mechanisms to limit CPU, memory, and I/O usage per database process or client connection.

Monitor and Audit:
Continuously track performance metrics and log query activities to ensure that rate limiting and throttling are effective, and periodically adjust thresholds based on observed load and threat patterns.

References:

  • OWASP API Security Project

standard.ref: DE.CM-1: The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
PR.DS-4: Adequate capacity to ensure availability is maintained
PR.PT-4: Communications and control networks are protected

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - M1035 - Limit Access to Resource Over Network

countermeasure.name: Implement secure backup procedures with encryption and access controls
countermeasure.desc:
Implement and regularly update secure backup procedures with encryption and access controls for the Other SQL Database to protect backup data from unauthorized access and tampering. This control ensures that all backup files are encrypted using strong cryptographic algorithms (e.g., AES-256) and that access to backup storage is strictly restricted through proper access controls. Developers and DevOps engineers should integrate automated backup tools with built-in encryption features and manage access via centralized policies to maintain data confidentiality and integrity.

Implementation Steps:

Encrypt Backup Files:
Configure backup tools to encrypt all backup files using robust encryption standards (e.g., AES-256) before they are stored or transmitted.

Enforce Access Controls:
Set strict file system permissions and use secure cloud storage access policies to ensure that backup files are accessible only to authorized personnel.

Automate Backup Processes:
Utilize automated backup solutions that enforce encryption and access controls consistently across all database instances.

Monitor and Audit:
Regularly review backup logs and conduct audits to verify that encryption and access policies are correctly applied and that no unauthorized access occurs.

References:

  • PostgreSQL Backup and Restore Documentation

standard.ref: Business Continuity Management and Operational Resilience

ID.AM-6: Cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for the entire workforce and third-party stakeholders (e.g., suppliers, customers, partners) are established
ID.BE-4: Dependencies and critical functions for delivery of critical services are established
ID.BE-5: Resilience requirements to support delivery of critical services are established for all operating states (e.g. under duress/attack, during recovery, normal operations)
ID.RA-4: Potential business impacts and likelihoods are identified

RS.CO-4: Coordination with stakeholders occurs consistent with response plans
RS.IM-1: Response plans incorporate lessons learned
RS.RP-1: Response plan is executed during or after an incident

countermeasure.name: Regularly update the database to the latest secure version
countermeasure.desc:
Implement and regularly update the database to the latest secure version to ensure that all known vulnerabilities are promptly patched and that the system benefits from the latest security improvements. This control minimizes the risk of exploitation by integrating an effective patch management process. Developers and DevOps engineers should monitor for new releases, test updates in a controlled environment, and deploy them consistently across all database instances using automated tools where possible.

Implementation Steps:

Monitor for New Releases:
Subscribe to security advisories and release notes from the database vendor to stay informed about updates and patches.

Test Updates in Staging:
Validate new versions in a staging environment to ensure compatibility and stability with existing applications before deploying to production.

Automate Deployment:
Use automated deployment tools or scripts to roll out updates uniformly across all database instances.

Audit and Verify Compliance:
Regularly review version inventories and update logs to confirm that all instances are running the latest secure version of the database.

References:

  • NIST SP 800-40 Revision 4: Guide to Enterprise Patch Management Technologies

standard.ref: Contract Requirements for Service Providers and Contractors (a)
ID.RA-1: Asset vulnerabilities are identified and documented
PR.IP-12: A vulnerability management plan is developed and implemented

countermeasure.name: Use parameterized queries and validate inputs
countermeasure.desc:
Implement and regularly update secure coding practices by using parameterized queries and validating inputs for the Other SQL Database to prevent SQL injection and other injection attacks. This control ensures that user-supplied data is properly sanitized and that SQL queries are constructed safely by separating query logic from data inputs. Developers and DevOps engineers should integrate these measures into the application layer interacting with the database, conduct regular code reviews, and utilize automated security testing tools to identify potential injection vulnerabilities.

Implementation Steps:

Adopt Parameterized Queries:
Ensure that all SQL queries are constructed using parameterized queries or prepared statements to separate data from query logic, preventing the injection of malicious code.

Validate and Sanitize Inputs:
Implement strict input validation routines to verify that all user-supplied data conforms to expected formats, and sanitize inputs to remove or escape potentially dangerous characters.

Integrate Secure Coding Practices:
Incorporate secure coding guidelines into the development process, and use automated tools and static code analysis to detect and remediate injection vulnerabilities.

Conduct Regular Code Reviews and Testing:
Perform periodic code reviews and penetration testing to verify that input validation and parameterized query practices are consistently applied across the application.

References:

  • OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet

standard.ref: PR.DS-6: Integrity checking mechanisms are used to verify software, firmware, and information integrity

usecase.name: Denial of Service
usecase.desc: Denial of Service attacks aim to disrupt the availability of services or resources to legitimate users. Attackers overwhelm systems with excessive traffic or requests, causing them to become unresponsive or unavailable to users.

threat.name: Attackers cause denial of service through resource exhaustion
threat.desc:
General Threat Description:
This threat occurs when attackers intentionally overwhelm an Other SQL Database by sending an excessive number of queries or performing resource-intensive operations. The resultant strain on CPU, memory, disk I/O, or network bandwidth can render the database unresponsive, effectively denying legitimate users access to the service.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors:

  • External attackers using automated tools or botnets to flood the database with a high volume of requests.
  • Malicious insiders triggering resource-intensive operations deliberately to disrupt service.
  • Adversaries exploiting inefficient query execution or misconfigurations to maximize resource consumption.
  • Distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks targeting specific database endpoints.

Impacts:

  • Complete or partial service unavailability, causing significant downtime.
  • Degraded database performance and increased query latency, affecting overall application responsiveness.
  • Financial losses due to operational disruptions and potential impact on business continuity.
  • Reputational damage from prolonged service outages and reduced customer trust.

Example Attack Scenarios:

  • An attacker uses a botnet to send an overwhelming number of complex SQL queries to the database, exhausting system resources and causing the database to crash.
  • A malicious insider deliberately executes resource-heavy operations during peak hours, leading to significant degradation in database performance and denial of service for legitimate users.

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - T1499 - Endpoint Denial of Service

usecase.name: Tampering
usecase.desc: Tampering refers to unauthorized modification of data or systems. Attackers may alter data in transit or modify software components to introduce vulnerabilities or compromise the integrity of the system.

threat.name: Attackers tamper with data due to insecure file permissions
threat.desc:
General Threat Description:
This threat occurs when PostgreSQL database files are stored with overly permissive file system permissions. Insecure file permissions allow unauthorized users or processes to access and modify critical database files, thereby undermining data integrity and confidentiality.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors:

  • External attackers exploiting weak file permissions through physical or remote access.
  • Insider threats abusing their legitimate access to alter or delete database files.
  • Malware that leverages misconfigured file systems to tamper with or corrupt data.

Impacts:

  • Unauthorized modification or deletion of critical data, leading to data corruption.
  • Compromise of database integrity and potential disruption of application functionality.
  • Increased risk of data breaches and non-compliance with data protection regulations.

Example Attack Scenarios:

  • An attacker gains access to a server with poorly configured file permissions, modifies transaction logs, and alters database records to cover up fraudulent activities.
  • A malicious insider exploits insecure file permissions to modify or delete sensitive customer data, resulting in significant operational disruption and reputational damage.

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - T1222 - File and Directory Permissions Modification

threat.name: Attackers inject malicious sql commands via sql injection
threat.desc:
General Threat Description:
This threat occurs when an application fails to properly validate or sanitize user inputs, allowing attackers to insert malicious SQL commands into queries. Such injection attacks can bypass authentication, manipulate or delete data, and compromise the integrity and confidentiality of the database.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors:

  • External attackers using automated tools or manual techniques to identify injection vulnerabilities in web forms or API endpoints.
  • Cybercriminals targeting applications with weak input validation to exploit underlying database queries.
  • Insider threats intentionally exploiting poorly sanitized inputs to manipulate data.
  • Adversaries leveraging misconfigured ORM frameworks or legacy code that fails to enforce proper parameterization.

Impacts:

  • Unauthorized access to sensitive data, leading to data breaches and privacy violations.
  • Data manipulation, corruption, or deletion, which can undermine data integrity and reliability.
  • Potential escalation of privileges, facilitating further compromise of the database and connected systems.
  • Financial and reputational damage due to compromised data security and operational disruption.

Example Attack Scenarios:

  • An attacker submits a crafted input through a vulnerable web form that bypasses login controls by injecting SQL commands, allowing the extraction of user credentials from the database.
  • An adversary targets a poorly coded API endpoint by injecting SQL fragments into query parameters, leading to unauthorized deletion of critical records and corruption of transactional data.

threat.name: Attackers exploit outdated database vulnerabilities
threat.desc:
General Threat Description:
This threat occurs when an Other SQL Database is running an outdated version that contains known security vulnerabilities. Without timely patches and updates, these vulnerabilities remain exploitable, enabling attackers to gain unauthorized access, execute arbitrary code, or disrupt service availability.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors:

  • External attackers using automated tools to scan for databases running outdated software.
  • Cybercriminals leveraging publicly documented vulnerabilities in unpatched database versions.
  • Insider threats exploiting legacy systems with known security issues.
  • Malware specifically designed to target vulnerabilities in outdated database components.

Impacts:

  • Unauthorized access to sensitive or confidential data stored in the database.
  • Execution of arbitrary code, leading to potential full system compromise.
  • Denial-of-service or disruption of database operations, affecting business continuity.
  • Increased risk of lateral movement within the network, potentially compromising additional systems.

Example Attack Scenarios:

  • An attacker identifies a database instance running an outdated version with a known vulnerability and uses an exploit to gain administrative access, allowing data exfiltration and modification.
  • A malware campaign targets unpatched databases, exploiting a known vulnerability to spread across multiple systems and install ransomware, leading to widespread service disruption.

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - T1190 - Exploit Public-Facing Application

usecase.name: Information Disclosure
usecase.desc: Information disclosure occurs when sensitive data is exposed to unauthorized parties. This can include unauthorized access to confidential information, such as personal data, financial records, or intellectual property.

threat.name: Attackers exfiltrate data due to insecure backup procedures
threat.desc:
General Threat Description:
This threat occurs when backup procedures for an Other SQL Database are not securely implemented, leaving backup files unencrypted or accessible to unauthorized users. Insecure backup processes expose sensitive data to attackers, who can exfiltrate the information without directly interacting with the live database.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors:

  • External attackers scanning for misconfigured backup repositories or cloud storage with weak access controls.
  • Insider threats with legitimate access to backup systems abusing their privileges.
  • Malware that targets unencrypted or improperly secured backup files for data extraction.
  • Automated scripts designed to detect and exploit insecure backup configurations.

Impacts:

  • Unauthorized disclosure of confidential or sensitive data, leading to privacy breaches.
  • Financial and reputational damage due to exposure of critical business or customer information.
  • Regulatory non-compliance and potential legal repercussions stemming from data breaches.
  • Extended security incidents requiring remediation and increased monitoring efforts.

Example Attack Scenarios:

  • An attacker identifies a misconfigured backup server that stores unencrypted backup files of the SQL database, accesses the server remotely, and exfiltrates sensitive customer data.
  • A malicious insider with access to the backup storage downloads backup files and transfers them to an external location, allowing unauthorized parties to reconstruct confidential records.

threat.name: Attackers intercept data due to unencrypted communications
threat.desc:
General Threat Description:
This threat occurs when data transmitted between an Other SQL Database and its clients is not encrypted. Without secure communication protocols in place, the data is vulnerable to interception, allowing attackers to capture, view, or manipulate sensitive information as it traverses the network.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors:

  • External attackers using man-in-the-middle (MITM) techniques to intercept network traffic.
  • Cybercriminals exploiting unsecured public or corporate networks.
  • Compromised network devices or rogue Wi-Fi hotspots that facilitate data sniffing.
  • Insider threats or unauthorized users with access to network infrastructure capturing unencrypted data.

Impacts:

  • Unauthorized disclosure of sensitive information such as user credentials, personal data, and proprietary business data.
  • Data integrity issues if intercepted data is modified before reaching its destination.
  • Increased risk of subsequent attacks if intercepted session tokens or authentication credentials are misused.
  • Potential regulatory non-compliance and reputational damage due to data breaches.

Example Attack Scenarios:

  • An attacker sets up a rogue Wi-Fi hotspot near a business location, intercepting unencrypted communications between the database and client applications, thereby capturing sensitive data transmitted in plain text.
  • A malicious insider taps into an unsecured corporate network segment, using packet sniffing tools to capture unencrypted database queries and responses, leading to unauthorized data access.

threat.name: Attackers exploit misconfigurations in database settings
threat.desc:
General Threat Description:
This threat occurs when an Other SQL Database is deployed with improper or default configurations that deviate from security best practices. Misconfigurations can include open network access, weak default settings, or improperly secured administrative interfaces, which provide attackers with opportunities to bypass security controls and compromise the database.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors:

  • External attackers using automated scanning tools to detect misconfigured database instances.
  • Cybercriminals exploiting default settings or weak configurations to gain unauthorized access.
  • Insider threats abusing misconfigurations to elevate privileges or access restricted data.
  • Adversaries leveraging misconfigured network access controls to intercept or manipulate database communications.

Impacts:

  • Unauthorized access to sensitive data and administrative functions.
  • Data corruption, deletion, or unauthorized modifications leading to compromised data integrity.
  • Service disruption, including downtime or degraded performance, affecting business continuity.
  • Increased risk of lateral movement within the network, potentially leading to broader system compromise.

Example Attack Scenarios:

  • An attacker scans for Other SQL Database instances with default settings and finds one with open access, enabling them to log in without proper credentials and extract sensitive information.
  • A malicious insider leverages a misconfigured database that permits overly broad administrative privileges, allowing them to modify critical data or disrupt service without triggering security alerts.

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - T1046 - Network Service Discovery

usecase.name: Spoofing
usecase.desc: Spoofing involves an attacker pretending to be someone else by falsifying data or identity. This can include spoofing email addresses, IP addresses, or user identities to gain unauthorized access to systems or information.

threat.name: Attackers gain unauthorized access due to weak authentication
threat.desc:
General Threat Description:
This threat arises when the authentication mechanisms for the Other SQL Database are insufficient, allowing attackers to bypass security measures. Weak authentication, such as easily guessable passwords, default credentials, or lack of multi-factor authentication, can provide attackers with a low barrier to unauthorized entry into the system.

Threat Agents/Attack Vectors:

  • External attackers using automated brute-force or credential stuffing techniques.
  • Insider threats leveraging default or weak credentials.
  • Adversaries exploiting misconfigured authentication settings or social engineering tactics to acquire valid credentials.

Impacts:

  • Unauthorized access to sensitive data and administrative functions.
  • Data manipulation, corruption, or deletion compromising the integrity of the database.
  • Increased risk of lateral movement within the network, potentially leading to broader system compromise.
  • Financial losses and reputational damage due to data breaches and security incidents.

Example Attack Scenarios:

  • An attacker employs a brute-force tool to systematically guess weak passwords on a database exposed to the internet, eventually gaining access to confidential customer information.
  • A malicious insider, aware of the default credentials on a newly deployed database, uses them to access and modify critical records without triggering security alerts.

weakness.name: CWE-285
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-285: Improper Authorization The product does not perform or incorrectly performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action.

weakness.name: CWE-287
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-287: Improper Authentication When an actor claims to have a given identity, the product does not prove or insufficiently proves that the claim is correct.

weakness.name: CWE-799
weakness.desc: CWE: CWE-799: Improper Control of Interaction Frequency The product does not properly limit the number or frequency of interactions that it has with an actor, such as the number of incoming requests. This can allow the actor to perform actions more frequently than expected. The actor could be a human or an automated process such as a virus or bot. This could be used to cause a denial of service, compromise program logic (such as limiting humans to a single vote), or other consequences. For example, an authentication routine might not limit the number of times an attacker can guess a password. Or, a web site might conduct a poll but only expect humans to vote a maximum of once a day.

countermeasure.name: Connectors should be provided for integrating with identity providers (IdPs)
countermeasure.desc:
To ensure seamless integration with identity providers and strengthen authentication mechanisms for your API gateway, follow these detailed steps:

  1. Evaluate and Choose an Identity Provider (IdP): Begin by evaluating well-known IdPs such as Auth0, Okta, Google Identity Platform, or Azure Active Directory. Consider factors like compatibility with your API gateway, support for OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect, scalability, and security features.
  2. Configure the API Gateway: Update your API gateway to support the identity standards endorsed by your chosen IdP. This often involves configuring the gateway to handle OAuth 2.0 authorization flows such as authorization code, implicit, or client credentials.
    1. Access your API gateway management console.
    2. Navigate to the security settings or authentication section.
    3. Enable support for JWT tokens or SAML assertions if applicable.
  3. Set Up Communication with the IdP: Establish a connection between your API gateway and the IdP by registering your API gateway as a client application with the IdP. Ensure that you:
    1. Register redirect URIs that the IdP can use to send authorization responses back to your application.
    2. Configure client IDs and client secrets in a secure manner, using environment variables or a secure server setup.
  4. Implement Authentication Flows: Depending on the features offered by the IdP, implement appropriate authentication flows on your API gateway. For example, support the PKCE extension for OAuth 2.0 to enhance security in public applications.
    1. Ensure the API gateway can initiate the proper OAuth flow and handle redirects appropriately.
    2. Configure scopes, claims, and resource access policies as required by your applications.
  5. Test the Integration: Conduct thorough testing by simulating various authentication scenarios. Verify that access tokens are issued and validated correctly, and that user information is accurately retrieved if using OpenID Connect.
    1. Check logs for any authentication errors or misconfigurations.
    2. Use testing tools like Postman or Curl to simulate authentication requests.
  6. Monitor and Adjust: Once integration is live, continuously monitor authentication logs for any unusual activity or failures. Be prepared to adjust configurations based on changes to IdP features or security guidelines.

References

  • Google Identity Platform - Authenticating Users
  • Azure Active Directory - Authentication Scenarios

countermeasure.name: Distributed gateway deployments should have a token translation (exchange) service between gateways
countermeasure.desc:
To facilitate secure token exchange across distributed API gateways and prevent unauthorized access, the following steps should be taken:

  1. Implement OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect:
    • Set up OAuth 2.0 to handle secure token issuance and validation. This protocol will manage access according to user authentication and application privileges.
    • Incorporate OpenID Connect to provide an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 for user verification.
  2. Use JSON Web Tokens (JWT):
    • Configure the gateways to use JWTs as a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties. This will enhance interoperability and speed of the token exchange.
    • Ensure all JWTs are signed and optionally encrypted. Use strong, up-to-date symmetric (HS256) or asymmetric (RS256) algorithms to secure token data.
  3. Secure Communication Channel:
    • Mandate TLS (Transport Layer Security) for all gateway communications. This will encrypt the data in transit, ensuring tokens cannot be intercepted by unauthorized parties.
  4. Token Expiration and Refresh:
    • Ensure that issued tokens have an expiration time to minimize the impact of a token being compromised.
    • Implement token refresh mechanisms to allow seamless renewal of tokens. Consider using refresh tokens with short expiration times for higher security.
  5. Distributed Trust and Key Management:
    • Establish a distributed trust model by designating key management infrastructure for token signing and validation across the gateways.
    • Implement automated key rotation policies and ensure that all nodes in the distributed gateway system are updated promptly to recognize new keys.
  6. Audit and Monitoring:
    • Integrate logging and auditing mechanisms to track token usage across the distributed gateways.
    • Use these logs to identify any unusual or unauthorized access patterns, which will help in timely detection and mitigation of security incidents.

Following these steps can help maintain the integrity, confidentiality, and availability of the API gateway system.

References

  • OAuth 2.0 Framework
  • OpenID Connect Introduction
  • Introduction to JSON Web Tokens
  • RFC 5246: The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol
  • RFC 7523: JSON Web Token (JWT) Profile for OAuth 2.0 Client Authentication and Authorization Grants
  • Keycloak Server Administration Guide

standard.ref: SC-12 CRYPTOGRAPHIC KEY ESTABLISHMENT AND MANAGEMENT

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - M1026 - Privileged Account Management

countermeasure.name: Integrate the API gateway with an identity management application
countermeasure.desc:
To effectively provision credentials before activating the API and prevent unauthorized access, follow these detailed steps:

  1. Identify Credential Requirements:
    • Determine the type of authentication your API will support (e.g., OAuth 2.0, API Key, JWT).
    • Define user roles and permissions associated with different levels of access to the API.
  2. Generate Secure Credentials:
    • Use a reliable and secure method to generate API keys or tokens. Consider using libraries like JWT for JSON Web Tokens.
    • Apply strong cryptographic practices like using a secure random function for generating secrets.
  3. Establish Credential Distribution Mechanism:
    • Set up an access control mechanism that ensures only authorized individuals or systems can request and receive credentials.
    • Utilize secure channels such as TLS/SSL to transmit credentials during distribution.
  4. Configure API Gateway:
    • Segregate the API activation stage to ensure credentials are mandatory before any API consumption.
    • Implement infrastructure configuration that enforces credential verification for each API request.
    • Consider using API management platforms like AWS API Gateway or Google Cloud API Gateway to streamline this process.
  5. Test Credential Mechanisms:
    • Conduct thorough testing on how credentials interact with your API, ensuring that unauthorized access is denied.
    • Regularly perform security audits to check for vulnerabilities in the credential provisioning and verification process.
  6. Monitor Credential Usage:
    • Implement logging mechanisms to track the usage of credentials and analyze access patterns.
    • Set up alerts for suspicious behavior or potential credential compromises, using tools like Datadog or Elastic Security.

Following these steps will help ensure a robust credential provisioning strategy, enhancing the security of your API gateway and protecting against unauthorized access.

References

  • OAuth 2.0 Getting Started
  • JWT Introduction
  • AWS API Gateway
  • Google Cloud API Gateway
  • Datadog
  • Elastic Security

countermeasure.name: Securely channel all traffic information to a monitoring and/or analytics application
countermeasure.desc:
Step 1: Define Monitoring Objectives

Begin by establishing clear objectives for what the continuous monitoring aims to achieve. Identify critical assets, potential threats, and the types of anomalies that need detection. Consider strategic goals such as improving detection speed, reducing false positives, and ensuring comprehensive coverage across all API endpoints.

Step 2: Select Monitoring Tools and Technologies

Evaluate and select appropriate tools and technologies for monitoring API traffic. Popular solutions include intrusion detection/prevention systems, security information and event management (SIEM) solutions, and anomaly detection tools. Ensure that selected solutions can integrate smoothly with the existing API gateway infrastructure.

Consider tools like AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, or Splunk which are highly capable in monitoring and analyzing API traffic effectively.

Step 3: Implement Traffic Logging and Analysis

Enable comprehensive logging of API requests and responses. Configure logs to capture metadata such as timestamps, source IP addresses, request URLs, HTTP methods, and response codes. Logs should be structured in a consistent format to facilitate easy analysis. Utilize log management solutions to collect, aggregate, and store logs securely for further examination.

Step 4: Develop Automated Alerting and Response Procedures

Create automated alerts for detected anomalies or potential security incidents. Configure the monitoring system to trigger alerts based on predefined thresholds and patterns. Develop response playbooks that dictate actions to be taken upon receiving alerts, ensuring a swift and organized response to incidents.

Consider integrating automated responses that can temporarily block malicious IPs or throttle suspicious activity to limit potential damage.

Step 5: Conduct Regular Threat Intelligence Update

Regularly update the monitoring system with the latest threat intelligence data. This includes integrating real-time threat feeds that provide updates on new vulnerabilities, emerging attack vectors, and other pertinent threat information. Collaborate with threat intelligence providers to enhance detection capabilities and adapt quickly to changing threat landscapes.

Step 6: Perform Continuous Improvement and Testing

Regularly test and refine the monitoring and response system. Conduct simulated attacks and penetration testing to evaluate the effectiveness of the implemented measures. Gather feedback and analytics to identify areas for improvement. Continuous refinement ensures that the system remains robust against evolving threats.

Step 7: Ensure Compliance and Reporting

Ensure that all monitoring and incident response activities comply with relevant regulations and standards, such as GDPR, HIPAA, or PCI-DSS. Develop comprehensive reporting mechanisms to document incidents and responses, which are valuable for audits and improving security posture over time.

References

  • AWS CloudWatch Documentation
  • Azure Monitor Documentation

countermeasure.name: The API gateway should have a connector to an artifact that can generate an access token for the client request
countermeasure.desc:
To enhance the security of the API gateway, it's crucial to implement a robust token generation mechanism. This process will ensure that only authorized clients can access secure resources. Here's a step-by-step guide to implement token generation for API client verification:

  • Choose a Token Standard:

Determine the type of token that fits your security requirements. Common choices are JSON Web Tokens (JWT), OAuth tokens, or custom tokens. For most modern applications, JWT is preferred due to its compact size and ease of use across platforms.

  • Set Up a Secure Token Generation Service:

Implement or integrate a token generation service. This service should handle the creation, signing, and expiration of tokens. When using JWT, consider a library like JWT.io for signing and verification across different programming languages.

  • Define Token Claims:

Decide on the necessary information (claims) the token should carry, such as issuer, subject, audience, and expiration. Ensure that these claims are minimal to protect against data leaks if a token is exposed.

  • Generate and Distribute Tokens:

Upon a successful authentication request, generate a token using your chosen library and send it securely to the client. Tokens should be sent via HTTPS to prevent interception.

  • Implement Token Validation:

At the API gateway, every incoming request should be checked for a valid token. Use your token service to validate its signature and claims. Reject requests with invalid or expired tokens.

  • Log and Monitor Token Activity:

Create logs for issuing, renewing, and rejecting tokens. Monitor these logs actively to detect any suspicious activities that could indicate an attempt to breach security.

  • Plan for Token Revocation:

Implement a mechanism to revoke tokens before their natural expiration if a security threat is detected or if a client logs out. Blacklist or short-lived token approaches can be beneficial.

Security Considerations:

  • Ensure your signing keys are securely stored, possibly using a hardware security module or a service like AWS KMS.
  • Use strong, commonly-accepted algorithms for signing tokens, such as RS256.
  • Regularly audit the token handling procedures for vulnerabilities.

References

  • OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework
  • Introduction to JSON Web Tokens
  • Secure your Java APIs with JWT
  • Learn About JSON Web Tokens
  • RFC 7519: JSON Web Token (JWT)

usecase.name: Repudiation
usecase.desc: Repudiation involves denying an action or event that has taken place. In a security context, repudiation threats can occur when a user denies performing a specific action, such as denying sending a message or making a transaction.

threat.name: Exploitation of insufficient logging and monitoring
threat.desc:
General threat description
The absence of adequate logging and monitoring allows attackers to execute malicious actions without detection. This can lead to unauthorized access and potential data breaches with the exploitation remaining hidden from defenders.
Threat agents/Attack vectors
Cybercriminals may exploit this weakness by injecting malicious code into the API gateway or by initiating a series of unauthorized requests. Without proper monitoring, these activities can go unnoticed.
Impacts
This threat can lead to compromised systems, unauthorized data access, and potential data loss or manipulation. Moreover, it may result in delayed response to breaches and increased recovery costs.
Example Attack Scenarios
An attacker successfully bypasses authentication controls and gains access to sensitive data through the API gateway. Due to the absence of logging, the attacker maintains access over an extended period, exfiltrating data without triggering alerts.

customField.value: ATT&CK Enterprise - T1089 - Disabling Security Tools

usecase.name: Elevation of Privilege
usecase.desc: Elevation of privilege involves an attacker gaining higher levels of access or permissions than intended. By exploiting vulnerabilities, attackers can escalate their privileges within a system to perform unauthorized actions or access sensitive data.

threat.name: Authentication bypass
threat.desc:
General threat description
An attack scenario in which malicious actors find ways to circumvent the authentication mechanisms, leading to unapproved access to API functionalities.
Threat agents/Attack vectors
Attackers may exploit vulnerabilities in the authentication process or use stolen credentials to gain access without proper authorization.
Impacts
Such actions can result in unauthorized data access, data breaches, or service misuse, compromising both security and privacy.
Example Attack Scenarios
An attacker could intercept unencrypted credentials or leverage a brute-force attack to bypass login controls, gaining entry to sensitive API endpoints without detection.

weakness.name: Defined security procedures are not followed
weakness.desc: Defined security procedures are not followed

weakness.name: Social engineering
weakness.desc: Innocent victim (e.g. owner, operator or maintenance engineer) being tricked into taking an action to unintentionally load malware or enable an attack
